title: Psalms 84
subtitle: How Lovely Is Your Dwelling Place

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “Better Is One Day in Your Courts”

heb.paragraph: "How lovely (yədîdūṭ) is your dwelling place, LORD Almighty!" (v.1). A Sons of Korah pilgrimage psalm. The soul yearns and even faints for God’s courts (v.2). "Even the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself near your altar" (v.3). The smallest birds have a home in God’s temple. "Blessed are those whose strength is in you, whose hearts are set on pilgrimage" (v.5). "They go from strength to strength, till each appears before God in Zion" (v.7).

notes.note: "How lovely is your dwelling place! My soul yearns, even faints, for the courts of the LORD." Total-person longing: soul and body cry out for the living God.
"Even the sparrow has found a home near your altar." If a sparrow is at home in God’s temple, so is the pilgrim.
"Blessed are those whose hearts are set on pilgrimage. They go from strength to strength." The journey itself is the blessing. Each stage brings more strength, not less.

refs.note: Many rooms in the Father's house and preparing a place fulfills the psalm's dwelling-place longing.
God's dwelling place among people completes the psalm's temple theology.
Dwelling in the LORD's house forever expresses the same desire for divine presence.

hist.context: Psalm 84 is a Korahite pilgrimage song expressing intense longing for the temple. The phrase 'how lovely is your dwelling place' (v.1) opens with the Hebrew mah-yedidot — 'how beloved.' Even sparrows and swallows find nests near the altar (v.3), intensifying the pilgrim's desire for such access to God's presence.

sections.header: Verses 8–12 — “Better Is One Day in Your Courts Than a Thousand Elsewhere”

heb.paragraph: "Better is one day in your courts (ḥāṣēr) than a thousand elsewhere; I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of the wicked" (v.10). The psalm’s most quoted verse. The ratio: 1 day with God > 1,000 days without. The lowest position in God’s house (doorkeeper) surpasses the highest position outside it. "The LORD God is a sun and shield; the LORD bestows favour and honour" (v.11).

notes.note: "Better is one day in your courts than a thousand elsewhere." The radical valuation: God’s presence is 1,000x more valuable than anything else. The doorkeeper’s position is better than the king’s tent.

"The LORD God is a sun and shield; he bestows favour and honour; no good thing does he withhold from those whose walk is blameless." Sun = light and warmth. Shield = protection. Favour + honour + no good thing withheld. Total provision.
"LORD Almighty, blessed is the one who trusts in you." The psalm’s closing beatitude. Trust in God is the definition of blessedness.

refs.note: Going from strength to strength despite weakness parallels Christ's power made perfect in weakness.
Longing for a better country with a prepared city develops the psalm's pilgrimage theology.
The Lamb shepherding and guiding to springs of living water fulfills the Baca valley's transformation.

hist.context: The 'Valley of Baka' (v.6, possibly 'weeping' or a balsam tree region) transforms into springs as pilgrims pass through — the journey itself becomes blessing. The declaration 'better is one day in your courts than a thousand elsewhere' (v.10) expresses the psalm's core conviction: divine presence surpasses any alternative.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " Total-person longing: soul and body cry out for the living God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVEven the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may have her young— a place near your altar, Lord Almighty, my King and my God.ESVEven the sparrow finds a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, at your altars, O LORD of hosts, my King and my God.NIVHear my prayer, Lord God Almighty; listen to me, God of Jacob.ESVO LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer; give ear, O God of Jacob! Selah

rec.text: ""How lovely is your dwelling place! My soul yearns, even faints, for the courts of the LORD." Total-person longing: soul and body cry out for the living God."

rows.label: One Day in Your Courts — 84:8–12
rows.text: "BETTER IS ONE DAY than a thousand elsewhere." Sun and shield. "No good thing withheld."

hebtext.note: "How lovely (yədîdūṭ) is your dwelling place, LORD Almighty!" (v.1). A Sons of Korah pilgrimage psalm. The soul yearns and even faints for God’s courts (v.2). "Even the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself near your altar" (v.3). The smallest birds have a home in God’s temple. "Blessed are those whose strength is in you, whose hearts are set on pilgrimage" (v.5). "They go from strength to strength, till each appears before God in Zion" (v.7).

"Better is one day in your courts (ḥāṣēr) than a thousand elsewhere; I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of the wicked" (v.10). The psalm’s most quoted verse. The ratio: 1 day with God > 1,000 days without. The lowest position in God’s house (doorkeeper) surpasses the highest position outside it. "The LORD God is a sun and shield; the LORD bestows favour and honour" (v.11).

themes.note: Worship peaks at 10: the supreme temple-longing psalm. Faith: trust as blessedness. Holiness: the dwelling place’s loveliness.
